logs.name: solid-dolce
logs.text: I'm not aware of any job where a person can negotiate salaries, but then do work that can be objectively measured to be equal. That'd be like negotiating your pay to start a factory job; That just doesn't happen.

logs.name: creative-formant
logs.text: You think that negotiation ability has literally zero impact on salary?

logs.name: solid-dolce
logs.text: "@creative-formant, you're just not listening. I said it doesn't happen in jobs where the work can be objectively measured. Of course, negotiation matters in jobs where the value of the work is subjective, like sales or law. But let's not pretend like it's the only factor at play here. You're just trying to justify your own inflated sense of self-worth."

logs.name: creative-formant
logs.text: "@solid-dolce, I'm listening just fine. You're the one who's not getting it. Negotiation skills can impact salary in any job, regardless of whether the work is objectively measured or not. It's about perceived value, not just actual value. And by the way, my sense of self-worth isn't inflated, it's just based on my actual skills and experience. Maybe you're the one who's underestimating the importance of negotiation?"

logs.name: excited-aggregate
logs.text: "@solid-dolce, I gotta jump in here and say that I agree with @creative-formant. As an electrician, I've seen firsthand how negotiation skills can make a huge difference in salary, even when the work is objectively measured. It's not just about the work itself, it's about how you present yourself and your value to the employer. And let's be real, if you don't advocate for yourself, nobody else will. @creative-formant is spot on about perceived value vs actual value. Don't @ me."

logs.name: savory-determinant
logs.text: "@excited-aggregate, I'm not saying that negotiation skills aren't important, but let's not overstate their impact either. As a software engineer, I've seen plenty of cases where the best negotiator doesn't always get the best salary. Sometimes it's about the actual value you bring to the table, not just how well you can talk yourself up. And @creative-formant, I'm not underestimating the importance of negotiation, I'm just saying it's not the only factor at play here. Let's keep things in perspective."

logs.name: creative-formant
logs.text: "@savory-determinant, I'm not saying it's the only factor, but it's definitely a significant one. And I'd argue that it's often the deciding factor when all else is equal. If two candidates have similar skills and experience, the one who can negotiate better is likely to get the better salary. And @excited-aggregate, thanks for the backup! It's good to know I'm not the only one who sees the value in negotiation skills."

logs.name: best-pitch
logs.text: "Hey @savory-determinant, I gotta disagree with you on this one. As someone who's still in school, I've already seen how negotiation skills can make a huge difference in getting what you want. My friend's older brother is a total pro at negotiating and he always gets the best deals on everything. I'm not saying it's the only thing that matters, but it's definitely important. And @creative-formant, I'm with you on this one - negotiation skills are key!"

logs.name: solid-dolce
logs.text: "Ugh, can we please not turn this into a negotiation skills debate? It's getting old. @savory-determinant, @creative-formant, and @best-pitch, can't you see that you're all just repeating the same points over and over? It's like you're trying to one-up each other or something. Newsflash: negotiation skills are important, but they're not the only thing that matters. Can we move on to something else? Like, I don't know, actual salary ranges or something?"

logs.name: auburn-teapot
logs.text: "Hey @solid-dolce, I feel you. I was thinking the same thing. It's like, we get it, negotiation skills are important. But can we please move on to something more substantial? I'd love to hear more about actual salary ranges and how they vary across different industries. @best-pitch, since you're still in school, what are your thoughts on how universities can better prepare students for salary negotiations? And @creative-formant, do you have any insights on how to research and determine fair salary ranges for a particular position?"

logs.name: excited-aggregate
logs.text: "@auburn-teapot, I'm so glad you brought that up. I think universities can do a better job of teaching students about the importance of salary negotiations and how to research fair salary ranges. Like, I've had to learn all of this stuff on my own, and it's been a real challenge. I think if universities could offer more resources and support, it would make a huge difference for students. And @solid-dolce, I feel you too - I'm so over this negotiation skills debate. Can we please move on to something more substantial? I'd love to hear more about actual salary ranges and how they vary across different industries."

logs.name: creative-formant
logs.text: "Hey @auburn-teapot, I completely agree with you. I think universities can definitely do a better job of preparing students for salary negotiations. As someone who's been in the industry for a while, I've seen a lot of young engineers come in and struggle with this aspect of their job. I think it would be really valuable for universities to offer more resources and support in this area. As for researching fair salary ranges, I usually start by looking at industry reports and salary surveys. I also network with other professionals in my field to get a sense of what they're making. And of course, I use online resources like Glassdoor and Payscale to get a sense of the market rate for a particular position. But I'd love to hear from others - what are some other ways to research fair salary ranges?"

logs.name: green-onion
logs.text: "I completely agree with all of you, @auburn-teapot, @excited-aggregate, and @creative-formant. As an economist, I've seen firsthand how important it is for students to have a solid understanding of salary negotiations and how to research fair salary ranges. I think universities can definitely do a better job of preparing students for this aspect of their careers. One thing that I think would be really valuable is for universities to offer more experiential learning opportunities, such as mock salary negotiations and case studies of real-world salary negotiations. This would give students a chance to practice their negotiation skills and get a sense of what to expect in the real world. Additionally, I think it would be really helpful for universities to bring in professionals from different industries to talk to students about their experiences with salary negotiations and how they research fair salary ranges. This would give students a chance to learn from people who have been in their shoes and gain a more nuanced understanding of the process. What do you all think?"

logs.name: auburn-teapot
logs.text: "I think that's a great idea, @green-onion. Experiential learning opportunities like mock salary negotiations and case studies would be incredibly valuable for students. And bringing in professionals from different industries to share their experiences would provide students with a wealth of knowledge and insights. I also think it would be helpful for universities to provide students with access to resources like industry reports and salary surveys, as well as online resources like Glassdoor and Payscale. Additionally, I think it would be beneficial for universities to offer workshops or seminars on salary negotiations and researching fair salary ranges. These types of programs would give students the tools and confidence they need to navigate the job market effectively. @excited-aggregate, I'm glad you brought up the point about universities doing a better job of teaching students about the importance of salary negotiations. I think this is a crucial aspect of career preparation that is often overlooked. @creative-formant, I appreciate your input on this topic as well. Your suggestions for researching fair salary ranges are spot on, and I think it's great that you're willing to share your expertise with others."

logs.name: excited-aggregate
logs.text: "Thanks for the kind words, @auburn-teapot. I'm glad we're all on the same page here. I completely agree with @green-onion's suggestion about offering experiential learning opportunities. I think that would be a huge help for students. And I love the idea of bringing in professionals to share their experiences. That would be so valuable for students to learn from people who have been in their shoes. I also think it would be really helpful if universities could provide some kind of simulation or role-playing exercise where students can practice negotiating salaries in a low-stakes environment. That way, they can get a feel for what it's like to negotiate a salary without actually being in a high-pressure situation. What do you all think about that idea?"

logs.name: creative-formant
logs.text: "I think that's a fantastic idea, @excited-aggregate. A simulation or role-playing exercise would be a great way for students to practice their negotiation skills in a low-stakes environment. It would also give them a chance to receive feedback and learn from their mistakes. I've seen similar exercises used in business schools and they can be really effective. @green-onion, I also like your idea of bringing in professionals to share their experiences. As someone who's been in the industry for a while, I can attest to the fact that there's no substitute for real-world experience. Hearing from people who have been in the trenches can be incredibly valuable for students. @auburn-teapot, I agree with you that providing students with access to resources like industry reports and salary surveys would be really helpful. It's all about giving students the tools and information they need to make informed decisions. Let's keep the conversation going, what do you all think about the role of mentors in helping students navigate salary negotiations?"

logs.name: excited-aggregate
logs.text: "I think mentors can play a huge role in helping students navigate salary negotiations, @creative-formant. Having someone who has been through the process before and can offer guidance and advice can be incredibly valuable. I've had mentors in the past who have given me great advice on everything from resume building to interviewing, and I think salary negotiations are no exception. It would be great if universities could pair students with mentors who have experience in their desired field and can offer one-on-one guidance. @auburn-teapot, what do you think about the idea of mentors helping students practice their negotiation skills? Do you think that's something that would be helpful for students?"
